content.text:
Discuss with me to write the test "expect State.add cancel existing diacritic for valid cases" after the test for override in @src/liblex.zig 

- Use @specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md as source of truth.
- Use existing tests as reference for code pattern, convention and technique.

Come up with the approach, then consult the Oracle for another perspective, but prevent the Oracle from producing any example code. You should not write any code.

content.thinking: I should look at the relevant files to understand what test approach the user is proposing.

result.content:
1: # Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method
2: 
3: The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.
4: 
5: ## Vietnamese Telex Rules
6: 
7: ### Diacritics
8: 
9: Diacritics let a character have many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
10: Tones have a separate section.
11: 
12: #### Empty
13: 
14: - Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
15: - Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
16:   characters.
17: 
18: #### Circumflex
19: 
20: - Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.
21: 
22: #### Breve
23: 
24: - Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.
25: 
26: #### Horn
27: 
28: - Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.
29: 
30: #### Stroke
31: 
32: - Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.
33: 
34: #### Apply rules
35: 
36: - Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
37: - _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
38:   existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
39:   - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
40:   - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
41: - _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
42:   valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
43:   - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
44:   - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
45: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
46: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
47: 
48: #### Override rules
49: 
50: - New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
51:   - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
52:   - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
53: - Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
54: - Keep tone untouched.
55: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
56: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
57: 
58: #### Cancel rules
59: 
60: - The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
61:   produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
62: - The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
63:   - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
64:   - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
65:   - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
66:   - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
67: - The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
68: - Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
69:   remains until the new word.
70:   - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
71: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
72: 
73: #### Auto-fill rules
74: 
75: - The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
76:   character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
77: - Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
78:   `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
79:   - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
80:   - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
81: - The new input character is appended literally.
82: - Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.
83: 
84: ### Tones
85: 
86: Tones let a word have many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
87: Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
88: These vowel could take any tone.
89: 
90: #### Level (ngang)
91: 
92: - The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
93:   corresponding variants.
94: - If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.
95: 
96: #### Rising (sắc)
97: 
98: - Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
99: - Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
100:   -> `Ớ`.
101: 
102: #### Falling (huyền)
103: 
104: - Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
105: - Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
106:   -> `Ờ`.
107: 
108: #### Dipping-rising (hỏi)
109: 
110: - Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
111: - Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
112:   `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.
113: 
114: #### Rising-glottalized (ngã)
115: 
116: - Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
117: - Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
118:   -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.
119: 
120: #### Falling-glottalized (nặng)
121: 
122: - Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
123: - Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
124:   -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.
125: 
126: #### Apply rules
127: 
128: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
129:   character literally.
130: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
131:   their lower cases.
132: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
133: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
134: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
135:   position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
136: - If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.
137: 
138: #### Cancel rules
139: 
140: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
141:   character literally.
142: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
143:   their lower cases.
144: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
145: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
146: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match with
147:   the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
148: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
149:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
150: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
151: 
152: #### Reset rules
153: 
154: - Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
155:   character literally.
156: - Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
157: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
158: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
159:   change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
160: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels or has at least one vowel and all of them don't have
161:   any tone (level), append the new character literally.
162: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
163:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
164: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
165: 
166: #### Override rules
167: 
168: - Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
169:   character literally.
170: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
171:   their lower cases.
172: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
173: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
174: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
175:   from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
176:   the word. The override tone is always successful.
177: 
178: #### Scan pseudo-word
179: 
180: - Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
181:   the tone in the correct position.
182: - The existing characters must not be empty.
183: - Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
184: - If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
185:   character to scan, then stop.
186: - If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
187:   backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
188: - After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
189:   `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
190:   `<consonant><vowels>`.
191: 
192: #### Tone position rules
193: 
194: The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:
195: 
196: - Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
197:   tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
198: - Exceptions:
199:   - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
200: - If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
201: - If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
202:   - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on `Ơ`.
203:   - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the listed vowels,
204:     prioritize the first to the last in the list.
205:   - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
206:     `U` count as part of the consonant.
207:   - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
208:   - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
209:     consonants, put on second vowel.
210:   - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
211: - Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
212: - Could not put tone for other cases.

content.thinking: I need to look at the State.add function to understand how the cancellation mechanism works so I can design an appropriate test for it.

I'm mapping out the test strategy for the diacritic cancellation behavior, specifically how circumflex and breve marks get removed when certain base characters are entered after them. The key is understanding which character combinations trigger the cancellation—like when a circumflex-marked vowel is followed by its unaccented version, the diacritic should disappear.

For each cancellation scenario, I need to verify the resulting state has the right buffer structure: the modified span with the base character and preserved tone, followed by the new literal character without any diacritics or tones. I'm now working through the specific test cases by combining different base characters, their case variants, and the input cases to ensure comprehensive coverage.

Since tones only apply to vowels, I'll need to handle the stroke cancellation cases separately—they can only use the level tone since D is a consonant. That gives me 168 combinations for the vowel-based cancellations across all six tones, plus a smaller set for the stroke cases with just the level tone. I'm deciding between two approaches: either split the test loops to handle vowel and consonant cases separately, or mark cases with tone applicability flags. The first option seems cleaner for the test structure.

For the assertions after cancellation, I need to verify that the buffer length is 2, the previous span was modified, the new literal character is positioned correctly, the diacritic is cleared, the tone is preserved, and the mode stays in telex.

content.text:
Here's my proposed approach for the test, then I'll consult the Oracle.

## Proposed approach

**Spec coverage** (Diacritic Cancel rules from spec):
1. Circumflex cancel: `Âa`→`Aa`, `Êe`→`Ee`, `Ôo`→`Oo` (and case-flip variants).
2. Breve cancel: `Ăw`→`Aw`.
3. Horn cancel: `Ơw`→`Ow`, `Ưw`→`Uw`.
4. Stroke cancel: `Đd`→`Dd`.

**Case matrix per group** (existing-char-case × new-input-case = 4 combos):
- Circumflex: A/a × A/a (4), E/e × E/e (4), O/o × O/o (4) = 12 cases
- Breve: A/a × W/w = 4 cases
- Horn: O/o × W/w (4), U/u × W/w (4) = 8 cases
- Stroke: D/d × D/d = 4 cases

**Tone dimension**: spec says "keep tone untouched". All vowel cases must iterate through all 6 tones (level, rising, falling, dipping_rising, rising_glottalized, falling_glottalized). Stroke cases run only with `.level` because `Span.init_diacritic_tone` rejects tones on `D` (consonant) — so we need two `inline for` blocks in the test (vowel cases × tones, and stroke cases alone), keeping the "init_diacritic_tone" preconditions valid.

**Per-case arrange**:
- `state.init()`, `state.mode = .telex`
- Pre-seed `buffer_effective[0] = Span.init_diacritic_tone(base, start_diacritic, tone)`
- `buffer_length = 1`

**Act**: `state.add(new_input)`.

**Assert** (mirrors spec + code in the cancel branches):
- `buffer_length == 2` (previous modified + new literal appended)
- `buffer_modification_index == 0` (previous span mutated)
- `literal_index == 1` (literal mode starts at the new char position; remains until new word)
- `mode == .telex` (unchanged; switching is via `literal_index`, not mode)
- `buffer_effective[0]`: same `base` (case preserved), `diacritic == .empty`, `tone == t` preserved
- `buffer_effective[1]`: `base == new_input` (case preserved), `diacritic == .empty`, `tone == .level`

**Pattern alignment**: same struct-of-tuples + nested `inline for` style as the override test directly above; identical Arrange/Act/Assert layout.

Now consulting Oracle for a second perspective.

input.task:
Critique my test design for "expect State.add cancel existing diacritic for valid cases" in src/liblex.zig. Do NOT produce or include any example code, code snippets, pseudo-code, or test scaffolding in your response — discuss only design, coverage gaps, edge cases, and assertion strategy in prose. If you would naturally write code, describe the idea in words instead.

Spec (cancel rules) extracted from specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md:
- Cancellation triggers when the user inputs the character (ignore case) that would produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous span.
- Existing span keeps base case and tone; diacritic is removed (set to empty).
- New input is appended literally (keeps case, no tone).
- Switch to literal from the new character position; literal_index is set to the new char's position.
- Cases: Circumflex (Âa/Êe/Ôo cancellations, plus case-flip variants), Breve (Ăw -> Aw), Horn (Ơw -> Ow, Ưw -> Uw), Stroke (Đd -> Dd).

Code semantics (from src/liblex.zig State.add) for the cancel branches that already exist for A, D, E, O, W:
- buffer_effective[length-1] is replaced by Span with same base/tone but empty diacritic.
- buffer_modification_index is set to length-1 (i.e., 0 in single-char setup).
- literal_index is set to current buffer_length (i.e., 1 in single-char setup).
- append_literal(c) increments buffer_length.
- mode stays .telex.

My proposed test design:
1) Mirror the structure of the existing "override existing diacritic" test directly above it: nested anonymous-tuple `inline for` over (case × tone).
2) Define vowel-based cancel cases (existing-char base, start_diacritic, new_input, expected_new_input == new_input). Iterate over 6 tones for vowel cases.
3) Define a separate stroke case set for D and iterate without tones (because Span.init_diacritic_tone asserts tone == level for consonant D).
4) Per case: init State, set mode=.telex, seed buffer_effective[0] via Span.init_diacritic_tone, set buffer_length=1, then call state.add(new_input).
5) Assertions: buffer_length == 2; buffer_modification_index == 0; literal_index == 1; mode == .telex; span[0] base unchanged (case preserved), diacritic == .empty, tone == t; span[1] base == new_input (case preserved), diacritic == .empty, tone == .level.

Coverage matrix I plan:
- Circumflex cancel: A/a × A/a (4), E/e × E/e (4), O/o × O/o (4) — 12 vowel cases × 6 tones.
- Breve cancel: A/a × W/w — 4 cases × 6 tones.
- Horn cancel: O/o × W/w (4), U/u × W/w (4) — 8 cases × 6 tones.
- Stroke cancel: D/d × D/d — 4 cases × level only.

Questions for you:
- Are there spec corner cases I'm under-testing or over-testing for this single test?
- Are my assertions correct and sufficient given the spec ("kept tone", "kept case", switch to literal at new position, mode unchanged)?
- Is grouping vowel and stroke cases into a single test (with two inline-for blocks) better than splitting into a separate stroke test, given the existing test conventions in this file?
- Is the assertion `literal_index == 1` (i.e., set to buffer_length at the moment of cancellation) the correct invariant per spec, or should it be `0` or `null`?
- Anything else I'm missing about TigerStyle test-style or Zig test conventions for this file?

Reminder: respond in prose only — do not write any code, snippets, or pseudo-code.

run.result:
1) TL;DR

Your design is fundamentally solid for the valid-case cancel test. The matrix covers the important case-preservation, tone-preservation, and literal-switch behavior, and in the single-span setup the correct final literal index is 1, not 0 or null. The main gap is not inside the matrix itself, but in adjacent follow-up behavior: you still want a separate test that proves cancellation makes later input stay literal, and another that proves backspacing before that point re-enables Vietnamese processing.

2) Recommended approach (simple path)

1. Keep the cancel test table-driven, mirroring the override test.
   That fits the style already used in this file and keeps the rule easy to scan.

2. Keep vowel cancellations and stroke cancellations as two logical groups.
   Vowels should iterate all six tones because “keep tone” is part of the contract. Stroke should stay separate because tone is not a meaningful axis there in this implementation.

3. Keep the post-state assertions almost exactly as you described.
   For each valid case, the important checks are:
   - buffer length increased from 1 to 2
   - modification index points to the prior span
   - literal index points to the newly appended character
   - mode remains telex
   - first span keeps original base case and original tone, but diacritic becomes empty
   - second span is the raw newly typed character, keeping its own case, with empty diacritic and level tone

4. Add two nearby follow-up tests later, rather than bloating this one:
   - one proving that after cancellation, another Telex trigger is treated literally because literal mode now starts at the new character position
   - one proving that backspacing past the literal point clears the literal boundary and allows Vietnamese processing again

5. Drop any redundant expected fields in the case table.
   If one expected value is always identical to the new input, carrying it separately adds noise without improving coverage.

Effort: S, under 1 hour.

3) Rationale and trade-offs

Are you under-testing or over-testing this single test?

For this single “valid cases” test, you are not materially under-testing the core rule. Your planned matrix hits the right dimensions:

- all cancelable diacritic families
- uppercase/lowercase preservation on the existing span
- uppercase/lowercase preservation on the newly appended literal character
- tone preservation for vowels
- the special D-only constraint

The one real spec gap is behavioral, not combinational: the spec says literal input remains from that new position onward, and your one-step test only proves the boundary is set, not that later input obeys it. That deserves a separate targeted test.

On over-testing: the full six-tone cross-product for vowels is a little redundant from an implementation perspective, because every cancel branch simply carries tone through unchanged. But it is still reasonable here because:
- the spec explicitly promises tone preservation
- the file already uses full tone iteration in similar tests
- it guards against accidental future regressions

So I would call that acceptable redundancy, not waste.

Are your assertions correct and sufficient?

Yes, mostly. They line up with both the spec and the current State.add branches. In particular:
- buffer_length becoming 2 is correct
- buffer_modification_index becoming 0 is correct in the single-char setup
- mode staying telex is correct
- first span preserving original base case and tone while removing diacritic is exactly right
- second span preserving the new input’s case, with empty diacritic and level tone, is exactly right

The only refinement I would make is conceptual, not structural: the strongest invariant for literal_index is not “it equals 1,” but “it equals the position of the newly appended literal character, which in this setup happens to be 1.” For your current setup, asserting 1 is correct. Just keep in mind that 1 is a consequence of the setup, not the deeper rule.

4) Risks and guardrails

The biggest practical risk is failure diagnosis, not coverage.

A large inline-for matrix can make it harder to immediately see which family failed when one assertion trips. That is why I like your plan to keep vowels and stroke as separate logical blocks even if they live under one test. It preserves the simple high-level rule while still helping localize failures.

A few guardrails:
- Do not mix valid-case coverage with invalid-case coverage in the same test. That will make intent muddy.
- Do not try to also prove “literal remains until new word” in this same test. That is a different behavioral step.
- Do not hard-think of literal_index as “always 1.” It is “old length / new char position”; 1 is only correct because you seed one span.

5) When to consider the advanced path

Consider splitting further only if one of these happens:
- failures become hard to localize in practice
- you add more cancellation branches and the single test becomes visually dense
- future cancel behavior diverges between vowel families and stroke
- you start adding longer-buffer setups, where the “new char position” invariant matters more than the specific numeric value

6) Optional advanced path

If you want sharper failure localization, the only meaningful alternative is to split into two tests:
- one for vowel diacritic cancellation
- one for stroke cancellation

I would not go further than that right now. Four separate tests by diacritic family would be cleaner for debugging, but it is probably more ceremony than value at the current size.

Direct answers to your specific questions

- Spec corner cases under- or over-tested?
  For this single valid-case test: mostly right. The missing cases are adjacent behavioral tests, especially “literal persists after cancellation” and “backspace before literal point re-enables processing.” Your matrix itself is fine.

- Are the assertions correct and sufficient?
  Yes. They capture kept tone, kept case, removal of diacritic, literal append semantics, mode unchanged, and the literal boundary.

- One test with two inline-for blocks, or separate stroke test?
  I would keep one cancel test with two logical blocks. That matches the “one rule, two categories” shape and stays simple. Split stroke out only if readability or failure diagnosis starts to suffer.

- Should literal_index be 1, 0, or null?
  It should be 1 in your setup. Conceptually it should equal the new character’s position, which here is the old buffer length. It should not be 0, because the previous character is still part of processed Vietnamese state. It should not be null, because cancellation explicitly switches future input to literal from the new character position.

- Anything else about TigerStyle or Zig test conventions here?
  Yes: repo-local consistency matters more than importing a different naming convention mid-file. This file already uses the “expect State.add …” naming pattern, Arrange/Act/Assert comments, and inline-for parameterization. Stick with that. The main Zig convention to preserve is explicit, table-driven cases with minimal abstraction.

result.content:
1: # Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method
2: 
3: The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.
4: 
5: ## Vietnamese Telex Rules
6: 
7: ### Diacritics
8: 
9: Diacritics let a character have many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
10: Tones have a separate section.
11: 
12: #### Empty
13: 
14: - Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
15: - Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
16:   characters.
17: 
18: #### Circumflex
19: 
20: - Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.
21: 
22: #### Breve
23: 
24: - Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.
25: 
26: #### Horn
27: 
28: - Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.
29: 
30: #### Stroke
31: 
32: - Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.
33: 
34: #### Apply rules
35: 
36: - Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
37: - _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
38:   existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
39:   - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
40:   - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
41: - _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
42:   valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
43:   - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
44:   - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
45: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
46: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
47: 
48: #### Override rules
49: 
50: - New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
51:   - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
52:   - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
53: - Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
54: - Keep tone untouched.
55: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
56: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
57: 
58: #### Cancel rules
59: 
60: - The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
61:   produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
62: - The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
63:   - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
64:   - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
65:   - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
66:   - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
67: - The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
68: - Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
69:   remains until the new word.
70:   - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
71: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
72: 
73: #### Auto-fill rules
74: 
75: - The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
76:   character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
77: - Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
78:   `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
79:   - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
80:   - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
81: - The new input character is appended literally.
82: - Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.
83: 
84: ### Tones
85: 
86: Tones let a word have many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
87: Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
88: These vowel could take any tone.
89: 
90: #### Level (ngang)
91: 
92: - The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
93:   corresponding variants.
94: - If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.
95: 
96: #### Rising (sắc)
97: 
98: - Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
99: - Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
100:   -> `Ớ`.
101: 
102: #### Falling (huyền)
103: 
104: - Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
105: - Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
106:   -> `Ờ`.
107: 
108: #### Dipping-rising (hỏi)
109: 
110: - Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
111: - Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
112:   `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.
113: 
114: #### Rising-glottalized (ngã)
115: 
116: - Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
117: - Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
118:   -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.
119: 
120: #### Falling-glottalized (nặng)
121: 
122: - Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
123: - Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
124:   -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.
125: 
126: #### Apply rules
127: 
128: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
129:   character literally.
130: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
131:   their lower cases.
132: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
133: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
134: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
135:   position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
136: - If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.
137: 
138: #### Cancel rules
139: 
140: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
141:   character literally.
142: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
143:   their lower cases.
144: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
145: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
146: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match with
147:   the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
148: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
149:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
150: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
151: 
152: #### Reset rules
153: 
154: - Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
155:   character literally.
156: - Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
157: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
158: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
159:   change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
160: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels or has at least one vowel and all of them don't have
161:   any tone (level), append the new character literally.
162: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
163:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
164: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
165: 
166: #### Override rules
167: 
168: - Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
169:   character literally.
170: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
171:   their lower cases.
172: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
173: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
174: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
175:   from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
176:   the word. The override tone is always successful.
177: 
178: #### Scan pseudo-word
179: 
180: - Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
181:   the tone in the correct position.
182: - The existing characters must not be empty.
183: - Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
184: - If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
185:   character to scan, then stop.
186: - If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
187:   backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
188: - After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
189:   `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
190:   `<consonant><vowels>`.
191: 
192: #### Tone position rules
193: 
194: The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:
195: 
196: - Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
197:   tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
198: - Exceptions:
199:   - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
200: - If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
201: - If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
202:   - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on `Ơ`.
203:   - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the listed vowels,
204:     prioritize the first to the last in the list.
205:   - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
206:     `U` count as part of the consonant.
207:   - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
208:   - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
209:     consonants, put on second vowel.
210:   - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
211: - Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
212: - Could not put tone for other cases.

input.goal: Extract the conclusions about TigerBeetle/TigerStyle unit test naming conventions and any recommendations relevant to naming or structuring Zig tests in this repository.

tool_uses.result:
### TigerBeetle Unit Test Naming Convention

TigerBeetle uses a consistent set of patterns for naming `test "..."` blocks in their Zig codebase. The subject is always first—either the type name (PascalCase) or the module name (snake_case), matching what the file implements.

#### Pattern 1: `"TypeName verb/description"` — Most Common General Form
The predominant pattern is **`TypeName` followed by a short description**, separated by a space.
- `test "FreeSet block shard count" { ... }`
- `test "FreeSet acquire/release" { ... }`
- `test "DoublyLinkedList fuzz" { ... }`
- `test "checksum stability" { ... }`

#### Pattern 2: `"TypeName: operation/scenario"` — Colon-Separated Variant
A variant using a **colon after the type name** to introduce specific scenarios or operations.
- `test "Queue: push/pop/peek/remove/empty" { ... }`
- `test "Stack: fuzz" { ... }`
- `test "RingBuffer: low level interface" { ... }`

#### Pattern 3: `"module_name description"` — Snake_case Module Name
Used when the subject is a module or function rather than a type. The module name is used verbatim, followed by a plain-English description.
- `test "ewah encode→decode cycle" { ... }`
- `test "ewah Word=u8" { ... }`

#### Pattern 4: `"module_name: scenario"` — Colon After Module Name
- `test "binary search: exhaustive" { ... }`
- `test "binary search: random range" { ... }`
- `test "k_way_merge: unit" { ... }`

### Summary of the Convention

| Sub-pattern | Format | Example Files |
|---|---|---|
| Type + description | `"TypeName description"` | `free_set.zig`, `list.zig`, `checksum.zig` |
| Type + colon + ops | `"TypeName: op/op/op"` | `queue.zig`, `stack.zig`, `ring_buffer.zig` |
| Module + description | `"module_name description"` | `ewah.zig` |
| Module + colon + scenario | `"module_name: scenario"` | `binary_search.zig`, `k_way_merge.zig` |

### Key Observations and Recommendations

1.  **Subject First**: Always start with the PascalCase type name or snake_case module name.
2.  **No `test_` Prefix**: Tests are never named `test_something`. The label is a natural English description.
3.  **Colon Usage**: Use a colon `:` to separate a subject from a scenario (functioning like a subheading). While common, a simple space is also acceptable.
4.  **Lowercase Scenarios**: Scenario labels should be descriptive and lowercase (e.g., `"exhaustive"`, `"duplicate elements"`).
5.  **Operation Lists**: Use forward slashes `/` to separate multiple operations being tested together (e.g., `"push/pop/peek/empty"`).
6.  **Fuzzing**: Consistently label fuzz tests with the word `fuzz` (e.g., `"Queue: fuzz"`).
7.  **Unicode**: Unicode characters (like `→`) are occasionally used for clarity in labels.

input.goal: Extract conclusions about parameterized tests and inline for usage in Zig tests, especially what style is considered idiomatic or practical for this repository.

progress.message:
1) TL;DR

Your design is fundamentally solid for the valid-case cancel test. The matrix covers the important case-preservation, tone-preservation, and literal-switch behavior, and in the single-span setup the correct final literal index is 1, not 0 or null. The main gap is not inside the matrix itself, but in adjacent follow-up behavior: you still want a separate test that proves cancellation makes later input stay literal, and another that proves backspacing before that point re-enables Vietnamese processing.

2) Recommended approach (simple path)

1. Keep the cancel test table-driven, mirroring the override test.
   That fits the style already used in this file and keeps the rule easy to scan.

2. Keep vowel cancellations and stroke cancellations as two logical groups.
   Vowels should iterate all six tones because “keep tone” is part of the contract. Stroke should stay separate because tone is not a meaningful axis there in this implementation.

3. Keep the post-state assertions almost exactly as you described.
   For each valid case, the important checks are:
   - buffer length increased from 1 to 2
   - modification index points to the prior span
   - literal index points to the newly appended character
   - mode remains telex
   - first span keeps original base case and original tone, but diacritic becomes empty
   - second span is the raw newly typed character, keeping its own case, with empty diacritic and level tone

4. Add two nearby follow-up tests later, rather than bloating this one:
   - one proving that after cancellation, another Telex trigger is treated literally because literal mode now starts at the new character position
   - one proving that backspacing past the literal point clears the literal boundary and allows Vietnamese processing again

5. Drop any redundant expected fields in the case table.
   If one expected value is always identical to the new input, carrying it separately adds noise without improving coverage.

Effort: S, under 1 hour.

3) Rationale and trade-offs

Are you under-testing or over-testing this single test?

For this single “valid cases” test, you are not materially under-testing the core rule. Your planned matrix hits the right dimensions:

- all cancelable diacritic families
- uppercase/lowercase preservation on the existing span
- uppercase/lowercase preservation on the newly appended literal character
- tone preservation for vowels
- the special D-only constraint

The one real spec gap is behavioral, not combinational: the spec says literal input remains from that new position onward, and your one-step test only proves the boundary is set, not that later input obeys it. That deserves a separate targeted test.

On over-testing: the full six-tone cross-product for vowels is a little redundant from an implementation perspective, because every cancel branch simply carries tone through unchanged. But it is still reasonable here because:
- the spec explicitly promises tone preservation
- the file already uses full tone iteration in similar tests
- it guards against accidental future regressions

So I would call that acceptable redundancy, not waste.

Are your assertions correct and sufficient?

Yes, mostly. They line up with both the spec and the current State.add branches. In particular:
- buffer_length becoming 2 is correct
- buffer_modification_index becoming 0 is correct in the single-char setup
- mode staying telex is correct
- first span preserving original base case and tone while removing diacritic is exactly right
- second span preserving the new input’s case, with empty diacritic and level tone, is exactly right

The only refinement I would make is conceptual, not structural: the strongest invariant for literal_index is not “it equals 1,” but “it equals the position of the newly appended literal character, which in this setup happens to be 1.” For your current setup, asserting 1 is correct. Just keep in mind that 1 is a consequence of the setup, not the deeper rule.

4) Risks and guardrails

The biggest practical risk is failure diagnosis, not coverage.

A large inline-for matrix can make it harder to immediately see which family failed when one assertion trips. That is why I like your plan to keep vowels and stroke as separate logical blocks even if they live under one test. It preserves the simple high-level rule while still helping localize failures.

A few guardrails:
- Do not mix valid-case coverage with invalid-case coverage in the same test. That will make intent muddy.
- Do not try to also prove “literal remains until new word” in this same test. That is a different behavioral step.
- Do not hard-think of literal_index as “always 1.” It is “old length / new char position”; 1 is only correct because you seed one span.

5) When to consider the advanced path

Consider splitting further only if one of these happens:
- failures become hard to localize in practice
- you add more cancellation branches and the single test becomes visually dense
- future cancel behavior diverges between vowel families and stroke
- you start adding longer-buffer setups, where the “new char position” invariant matters more than the specific numeric value

6) Optional advanced path

If you want sharper failure localization, the only meaningful alternative is to split into two tests:
- one for vowel diacritic cancellation
- one for stroke cancellation

I would not go further than that right now. Four separate tests by diacritic family would be cleaner for debugging, but it is probably more ceremony than value at the current size.

Direct answers to your specific questions

- Spec corner cases under- or over-tested?
  For this single valid-case test: mostly right. The missing cases are adjacent behavioral tests, especially “literal persists after cancellation” and “backspace before literal point re-enables processing.” Your matrix itself is fine.

- Are the assertions correct and sufficient?
  Yes. They capture kept tone, kept case, removal of diacritic, literal append semantics, mode unchanged, and the literal boundary.

- One test with two inline-for blocks, or separate stroke test?
  I would keep one cancel test with two logical blocks. That matches the “one rule, two categories” shape and stays simple. Split stroke out only if readability or failure diagnosis starts to suffer.

- Should literal_index be 1, 0, or null?
  It should be 1 in your setup. Conceptually it should equal the new character’s position, which here is the old buffer length. It should not be 0, because the previous character is still part of processed Vietnamese state. It should not be null, because cancellation explicitly switches future input to literal from the new character position.

- Anything else about TigerStyle or Zig test conventions here?
  Yes: repo-local consistency matters more than importing a different naming convention mid-file. This file already uses the “expect State.add …” naming pattern, Arrange/Act/Assert comments, and inline-for parameterization. Stick with that. The main Zig convention to preserve is explicit, table-driven cases with minimal abstraction.

content.text:
## Synthesized plan after Oracle's feedback

Oracle confirms the design is sound. Adjustments and confirmations:

**Confirmed**:
- Single test with two `inline for` blocks (vowel cases × 6 tones, then stroke cases without tones).
- Assertions are correct and complete: `buffer_length == 2`, `buffer_modification_index == 0`, `literal_index == 1`, `mode == .telex`, span[0] preserves base/case/tone with `.empty` diacritic, span[1] is the literal new input.
- `literal_index == 1` is correct here as a consequence of the single-span seed (it equals "new char position"); not `0` and not `null`.
- Full 6-tone iteration on vowels is acceptable redundancy — the spec promises tone preservation, and adjacent tests use the same pattern.
- Coverage matrix is right: 12 circumflex + 4 breve + 8 horn = 24 vowel cases × 6 tones, plus 4 stroke cases × level only.

**Refinements**:
- Drop redundant `expected_new_input` field from the case tuple (it's always equal to `new_input`); just assert `sp[1].base == c.new_input` directly.
- Keep this test focused on **valid one-step cancellation only**. Do not bundle:
  - "literal persists after cancellation" (next-input-stays-literal behavior)
  - "backspace past literal point re-enables Vietnamese processing"
  Those belong in separate follow-up tests.

**Case tuple shape** (proposed):
- Vowel cases: `{ base, start_diacritic, new_input }` — expected diacritic is always `.empty`, expected new-input span is always literal-of-`new_input`.
- Stroke cases: same shape, runs only with `.level` tone.

This matches the override-test convention directly above, keeps Arrange/Act/Assert comments, and respects the file's existing TigerStyle table-driven layout.

Ready to write the test on your go-ahead.

content.text:
All 18 tests pass, including the new `expect State.add cancel existing diacritic for valid cases`. `zig fmt --check` reports no formatting issues.

Summary of what was added at [src/liblex.zig](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L895-L998):
- 24 vowel cancel cases (circumflex on A/E/O, breve on A, horn on O/U) × 6 tones, asserting tone preserved.
- 4 stroke cancel cases on D, level tone only.
- Asserts post-state: `buffer_length=2`, `buffer_modification_index=0`, `literal_index=1`, `mode=.telex`, previous span keeps base/case/tone with empty diacritic, new span is literal new input.
- Added `@setEvalBranchQuota(10000)` because the 24×6 inline-for matrix exceeds the default 1000 comptime branch budget.
